papers.title: Tailoring Strength and Ductility of an Fe–Mn–Al–C Low-Density Duplex Steel by Controlling the Cooling Path after Hot Rolling
papers.paper_scope: Investigating the effect of cooling path after hot rolling on microstructure and mechanical properties of a low-density duplex Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C steel to achieve a better strength-ductility match via grain refinement and TRIP effect.

alloys.alloys_notes: Low-density duplex steel; composition given in wt%.

processes.description: Hot rolled with 7 passes at 900–1100°C to 4 mm thickness after reheating at 1200°C for 2 h, followed by air cooling to ambient temperature.
Hot rolled with 7 passes at 900–1100°C to 4 mm thickness after reheating at 1200°C for 2 h, followed by furnace cooling to ambient temperature.
Hot rolled with air cooling, then annealed at 800°C for 30 min.
Hot rolled with air cooling, then annealed at 900°C for 30 min.
Hot rolled with air cooling, then annealed at 1000°C for 30 min.
Hot rolled with furnace cooling, then annealed at 800°C for 30 min.
Hot rolled with furnace cooling, then annealed at 900°C for 30 min.
Hot rolled with furnace cooling, then annealed at 1000°C for 30 min.

processing_steps.processing_steps_notes: Forged billet reheated before hot rolling.
Seven passes, starting from 60 mm cross-section.
Forged billet reheated before hot rolling.
Seven passes, starting from 60 mm cross-section.
Cooled to ambient temperature in furnace.

structures.overall_structure: Banded morphology along rolling direction, consisting of δ-ferrite, austenite and martensite.

phases_present.morphology: Banded, with many low-angle grain boundaries due to recovery and recrystallization
Prior austenite bands, partially transformed to martensite

defects.lattice_distortion: High GND density due to martensitic transformation

phases_present.morphology: Mainly twin martensite, large distortion (dark IQ regions)

structures.overall_structure: Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite with a small amount of martensite.

phases_present.morphology: Prior austenite bands, small amount of martensite formed

defects.lattice_distortion: Lower GND density compared to AC

structures.overall_structure: Duplex phase of austenite and ferrite; no martensite. Needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite band refines austenite grains.

phases_present.morphology: Banded, with many low-angle grain boundaries (length 4351.1 μm)
Needle-like, formed by degradation of prior martensite; refines austenite grains
Film-like between needle-like α-ferrite, refined grains

structures.overall_structure: Duplex austenite and ferrite, similar to AC-A800. Needle-like α-ferrite refines austenite.

phases_present.morphology: Banded, low-angle grain boundary length 2685.4 μm

structures.overall_structure: Duplex austenite-ferrite, with reversed transformation of martensite dominant; small fraction of α-ferrite.

phases_present.morphology: Banded, partially recrystallized, low-angle grain boundary length 1634.3 μm

structures.overall_structure: Banded δ-ferrite, austenite, and small fraction of martensite; some fine equiaxed α-ferrite formed.

phases_present.morphology: Banded, with recovery; darker IQ indicating distortion
Fine equiaxed grains within austenite band
Coarse grains, larger than AC annealed samples
Dark regions in austenite band, formed during cooling after annealing

defects.lattice_distortion: Large distortion as reflected by darker IQ maps

structures.overall_structure: Banded δ-ferrite, austenite, and martensite; some equiaxed α-ferrite.
Banded δ-ferrite, austenite, and martensite; equiaxed α-ferrite present.

defect_interaction.quality_description: Dislocations pile up at the interface, causing strain concentration and cracking; also delamination of banded microstructure.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Strain concentration at martensite–austenite interface leads to crack initiation.

interfaces.phase_evolution: During cooling after annealing of furnace-cooled samples, part of austenite transforms to martensite due to lower stability from larger grain size.

tensile_properties.stress_strain_curves: Engineering stress-strain curve in Figure 9a (AC curve); higher strength but lower ductility than FC.
Engineering stress-strain curve in Figure 9b (FC curve); lower strength, higher ductility than AC.
Figure 9a (curve AC800); strong TRIP effect and work hardening rate recovery at Stage III.
Figure 9a (curve AC900); work hardening rate recovery at Stage III.
Figure 9a (curve AC1000); work hardening rate recovery at Stage III.
Figure 9b (curve FC800); abrupt fracture, no necking.

characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructural observation of hot-rolled and annealed samples, and tensile fracture surfaces
characterization_methods.key_findings: Revealed banded microstructure, phases (δ-ferrite, austenite, martensite, α-ferrite), needle-like α-ferrite after annealing, dimple fracture in AC-A800 and cleavage-dominated brittle fracture in FC-A800.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase maps, grain boundary distribution, kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps
characterization_methods.key_findings: AC hot-rolled sample: austenite KAM 0.48°, FC: 0.43°; low-angle grain boundary lengths in δ-ferrite decreased with annealing temperature; grain size distributions obtained.

characterization_methods.purpose: Measurement of austenite volume fraction before and after deformation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite fractions: AC-A800 58.6%, AC-A900 61.4%, AC-A1000 57.1%; FC-A800 62%, FC-A900 58.6%, FC-A1000 60.6%; after deformation large fraction consumed in AC annealed samples.

characterization_methods.purpose: Evaluation of mechanical properties (strength, ductility, work hardening)
characterization_methods.key_findings: Air-cooled annealed samples show higher yield strength (427-513 MPa) and superior elongation (>30%); furnace-cooled annealed samples have lower strength (267-419 MPa) and elongation <20%; work hardening rate recovery observed only in AC annealed samples. Strain rate 10^-3 s^-1, gauge length 50 mm, width 12.5 mm, rolling direction.

analytical_model.equation_role: Estimate Ms temperature considering composition and austenite grain volume to explain presence/absence of martensite after annealing
analytical_model.parameters: w_Mn, w_Al, w_C (weight percent); B = 613 μm K^-1; V_γ (austenite grain volume)
analytical_model.model_note: Formula: Ms = 692 - 37*w_Mn + 20*w_Al - 502*w_C^(1/2) - B*V_γ^(-1/3). Used to calculate upper limit Ms temperatures for AC annealed samples (24, 1, 34°C respectively) confirming stability of austenite.

analytical_model.equation_role: Qualitatively explain yield strength enhancement from grain refinement and dislocation density
analytical_model.parameters: k (Hall-Petch coefficient), d (grain size), M (Taylor factor), α=0.2, G (shear modulus), b (Burgers vector), ρ (dislocation density)
analytical_model.model_note: Δσ_P-H = k*d^(1/2); Δσ_d = M*α*G*b*ρ^(1/2). Grain refinement from 8-10 μm to 3-6 μm and higher GND density contributed to increased yield strength.

unmapped_findings: The studied steel was produced by vacuum induction melting, cast into an ingot, forged at 1050°C and homogenized for 2 h at 1050°C before reheating and hot rolling.
Fracture surface of AC-A800 showed dimples indicating high ductility, while FC-A800 exhibited cracking at martensite–austenite interfaces and cleavage-dominated brittle fracture.
During annealing of air-cooled samples, carbon and manganese partition into austenite, increasing chemical stability; needle-like α-ferrite further refines austenite, enhancing mechanical stability.
In furnace-cooled annealed samples, cracks are easily nucleated at the martensite–austenite interface, and delamination of banded microstructure leads to brittle fracture.
XRD measurements of austenite fraction after deformation: AC-A800 23.7%, AC-A900 44.4%, AC-A1000 46.4%; FC-A800 56.5%, FC-A900 52.4%, FC-A1000 52.1%.
The KAM values of austenite in hot-rolled AC and FC samples are 0.48° and 0.43°, respectively, indicating higher dislocation density in AC due to martensitic transformation.
Low-angle grain boundary length in δ-ferrite of AC-A800: 4351.1 μm, AC-A900: 2685.4 μm, AC-A1000: 1634.3 μm, showing progressive recovery with annealing temperature.
The TRIP effect of austenite played a critical role in the work hardening rate recovery (Stage III) observed only in air-cooled annealed samples, leading to a better strength-ductility match.
